summary:
Endocrine Emergencies

Diabetes Mellitus Overview

Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder characterized by hyperglycemia resulting from defects in insulin secretion, insulin action, or both. The two main types:

  • Type 1: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells → absolute insulin deficiency. Onset typically in childhood or adolescence. Patients are dependent on exogenous insulin and prone to DKA.
  • Type 2: Insulin resistance with relative insulin deficiency. Onset typically in adulthood (though increasing in younger populations). Managed with oral medications, diet, and sometimes insulin. More prone to HHS.

Hypoglycemia vs Hyperglycemia — Comparison

FeatureHypoglycemia (Low Blood Sugar)Hyperglycemia (High Blood Sugar)
OnsetRapid (minutes to hours)Slow (hours to days)
Blood glucose<70 mg/dL>250 mg/dL (DKA); >600 mg/dL (HHS)
SkinPale, cool, clammy (diaphoretic)Warm, dry, flushed
MentationConfusion, agitation, coma (early CNS effects)Drowsy, lethargic, coma (late CNS effects)
BreathingNormal or shallowDeep, rapid (Kussmaul respirations in DKA)
Breath odorNormalFruity/acetone (DKA with ketones)
Thirst/urinationNormalPolyuria, polydipsia (frequent urination, extreme thirst)
Heart rateTachycardia (sympathetic response)Tachycardia (dehydration)
Common triggersMissed meal, excess insulin, exerciseInfection, missed insulin, illness (sick day)
Clinical Pearl: If you cannot differentiate hypoglycemia from hyperglycemia in an altered patient with diabetes, treat for hypoglycemia. It is more immediately dangerous (brain damage can occur within 30 minutes) and can be rapidly corrected. Hyperglycemia develops slowly and is less immediately life-threatening.

Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)

DKA is a life-threatening complication of diabetes (more common in Type 1) characterized by hyperglycemia, ketosis, and metabolic acidosis. It develops over hours to days, often triggered by infection, missed insulin, or stress. Key features:

  • Blood glucose typically 250-500 mg/dL (can be higher)
  • Polyuria, polydipsia, and polyphagia (the "three P's")
  • Kussmaul respirations — Deep, rapid breathing as the body attempts to blow off CO₂ to compensate for acidosis
  • Fruity (acetone) breath odor — From ketones (acetoacetate) in the blood
  • Nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain — common presenting symptoms
  • Warm, dry, flushed skin from dehydration
  • Altered mental status ranging from alert to coma (correlates with severity)

Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS)

HHS is a life-threatening complication seen in Type 2 diabetes. It presents with extreme hyperglycemia (>600 mg/dL) and severe dehydration but without significant ketosis or acidosis. Onset is slower (days to weeks) than DKA. Key features:

  • Blood glucose often >600 mg/dL (can exceed 1,000 mg/dL)
  • Profound dehydration — dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, hypotension, tachycardia
  • Altered mental status common (lethargy, confusion, coma) — correlates with serum osmolality
  • No Kussmaul respirations (no acidosis)
  • No fruity breath odor (no ketones)
  • Common triggers: infection, stroke, MI, non-adherence to medications, corticosteroids

Blood Glucose Monitoring

EMS providers are trained to measure capillary blood glucose using a glucometer. Technique matters:

  • Use the side of the fingertip (fewer nerve endings, less painful)
  • Wipe away the first drop of blood (contains interstitial fluid that may dilute the sample)
  • Use the second drop for testing
  • Clean the site with alcohol and allow it to dry completely before pricking
  • Document the reading and the time it was obtained
  • Check blood glucose in ALL patients with: altered mental status, seizure, stroke-like symptoms, or known diabetes with any complaint

EMS Provider Management of Diabetic Emergencies

Hypoglycemia — awake and able to swallow:

  • Administer oral glucose (15-20 grams) — typically a single tube of glucose gel squeezed into the buccal pouch (between cheek and gum)
  • Encourage the patient to swallow slowly; monitor for airway compromise
  • Recheck blood glucose in 10-15 minutes; may repeat once per protocol
  • Transport for evaluation, even if symptoms resolve

Hypoglycemia — unconscious or unable to swallow:

  • Do NOT give oral glucose — aspiration risk
  • Administer glucagon IM (1 mg intramuscularly) — typically in the deltoid or anterolateral thigh
  • Position the patient in the recovery position (lateral recumbent)
  • Monitor for return of consciousness (usually within 5-15 minutes)
  • Transport — the effects of glucagon are temporary; blood glucose will drop again

Hyperglycemia (DKA/HHS):

  • Support airway, breathing, and circulation
  • Provide oxygen if hypoxic
  • Transport for definitive care (IV fluids, insulin, electrolyte management)
  • Do NOT give oral glucose (blood glucose is already high)
  • Do NOT give insulin (out-of-hospital insulin administration is not in the EMT scope)

Sick Day Rules for Diabetic Patients

  • Check blood glucose more frequently (every 2-4 hours)
  • Stay hydrated with sugar-free or low-sugar fluids
  • Continue taking insulin — may need dose adjustment; NEVER stop insulin entirely
  • If oral diabetes medications are used, continue as prescribed unless instructed otherwise
  • If vomiting, unable to eat, or blood glucose persistently >250 mg/dL, seek medical attention
  • Have a plan for ketone testing if prescribed
  • Know when to call the healthcare provider — worsening symptoms, fever >101°F, persistent vomiting

EMS providers should be aware that non-adherence to sick day rules is a common precipitant for DKA and HHS.

Altered Mental Status and Diabetes

Altered mental status in a patient with diabetes is a diabetic emergency until proven otherwise. Key assessment points:

  • Check blood glucose IMMEDIATELY as part of the primary assessment
  • Obtain a history of diabetes type, usual medications, last meal, recent illness
  • Look for a medical alert bracelet/necklace
  • If blood glucose is low (<70 mg/dL) and patient has an altered mental status, treat immediately
  • If blood glucose is high (>250 mg/dL), assess for signs of DKA (Kussmaul, fruity breath, abdominal pain) vs HHS (profound dehydration, no ketosis)
  • Document blood glucose readings and time of administration of any treatment

quiz.q: A 45-year-old female with Type 1 diabetes is found unconscious. Her skin is warm and dry, and her breath has a fruity odor. Her respiratory rate is 30 and her breaths are deep. Which condition is most likely?
quiz.explanation: Warm dry skin, fruity breath, and deep rapid (Kussmaul) respirations in a Type 1 diabetic are classic for DKA with compensation for metabolic acidosis.

quiz.q: An EMS provider administers oral glucose to a confused but conscious diabetic patient. Ten minutes later, the patient becomes unconscious. What is the EMS provider's next action?
options: Wait another 10 minutes for the glucose to take effect
Administer oral glucose again since the first dose may have been insufficient
Protect the airway, administer glucagon IM, and begin transport
Start CPR and prepare the AED
quiz.explanation: If a patient loses consciousness after oral glucose, protect the airway, give glucagon IM (since the patient can no longer safely swallow), and transport.

quiz.q: [HARD] A 62-year-old male with Type 2 diabetes is found lethargic with severe dehydration. His blood glucose is 940 mg/dL. He has no Kussmaul respirations and no fruity breath odor. Which condition is MOST consistent with these findings?
quiz.explanation: Extreme hyperglycemia (>600) with severe dehydration but WITHOUT ketosis/acidosis (no Kussmaul, no fruity breath) in a Type 2 diabetic is classic for HHS.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between DKA and HHS?
flashcards.answer: DKA: hyperglycemia + ketosis + acidosis, rapid onset (hours-days), Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath, seen in Type 1. HHS: extreme hyperglycemia (>600) without ketosis, slower onset (days-weeks), profound dehydration, seen in Type 2.

flashcards.question: What are the classic signs of hypoglycemia?
flashcards.answer: Rapid onset, pale cool clammy skin, tachycardia, confusion/agitation, hunger, tremor, weakness. Can progress to unconsciousness and seizures if untreated. Blood glucose <70 mg/dL.

flashcards.question: At what blood glucose level is hypoglycemia defined, and what is the treatment for an awake patient?
flashcards.answer: Hypoglycemia is defined as blood glucose <70 mg/dL. For an awake patient able to swallow: administer oral glucose gel (15-20 g) into the buccal pouch. Recheck in 10-15 minutes; may repeat once per protocol.

flashcards.question: How is glucagon administered and when is it indicated?
flashcards.answer: Glucagon 1 mg is administered IM (intramuscularly) in the deltoid or anterolateral thigh. It is indicated for unconscious hypoglycemic patients who cannot safely take oral glucose. Onset is 5-15 minutes.

flashcards.question: What are Kussmaul respirations and what do they indicate?
flashcards.answer: Deep, rapid breathing (panting-like pattern) that represents a compensatory respiratory alkalosis for metabolic acidosis. Classic finding in DKA — the body attempts to blow off CO₂ to correct the acidosis.

flashcards.question: What are the 'three P's' of hyperglycemia?
flashcards.answer: Polyuria (frequent urination), Polydipsia (extreme thirst), Polyphagia (increased hunger despite weight loss). Caused by osmotic diuresis from high blood glucose.

flashcards.question: What are 'sick day rules' for diabetic patients?
flashcards.answer: Check blood glucose more frequently, stay hydrated, continue insulin (never stop entirely), seek medical help if vomiting/unable to eat or if blood glucose >250 mg/dL. Fever >101°F or worsening symptoms require provider contact.

flashcards.question: What is the most important first step when assessing an altered patient with diabetes?
flashcards.answer: Check blood glucose immediately as part of the primary assessment. Hypoglycemia can cause permanent brain damage within 30 minutes and is rapidly reversible with treatment. Treat low blood sugar first, worry about the cause second.

critical.scenario: You respond to a 28-year-old female with Type 1 diabetes found by her roommate. The roommate reports the patient was acting confused and irritable about 20 minutes ago and then became unresponsive. On exam, the patient is unconscious, breathing shallowly at 14/min, with pale, cool, diaphoretic skin. Her pulse is 112 and bounding. A medical alert bracelet indicates Type 1 diabetes. You quickly check her blood glucose and it reads 32 mg/dL.
critical.question: What is the correct management for an unconscious hypoglycemic patient?

options.t: Administer oral glucose gel into the buccal pouch despite unconsciousness
options.f: Incorrect. Never give oral glucose to an unconscious patient — high aspiration risk. Oral glucose requires the patient to be awake and able to swallow.

options.t: Open the airway, position in recovery position, administer glucagon 1 mg IM in the anterolateral thigh, monitor for return of consciousness, and transport
options.f: Correct. Glucagon IM is the appropriate treatment for unconscious hypoglycemia. The patient should begin to awaken within 5-15 minutes.

critical.explanation: Unconscious hypoglycemia requires glucagon IM — never oral glucose. Position in recovery position, administer glucagon, monitor airway, and transport. Effects of glucagon are temporary.

critical.scenario: You are called to a 20-year-old male with Type 1 diabetes who has been vomiting for 2 days with abdominal pain. His friends say he ran out of insulin 3 days ago and hasn't been able to refill his prescription. On exam, he is drowsy and confused. His skin is warm, dry, and flushed. His respiratory rate is 32 with very deep breaths. You note a fruity odor on his breath. His blood glucose is 480 mg/dL. His SpO₂ is 97% on room air.
critical.question: What condition is this patient most likely experiencing, and what is the appropriate prehospital management?

options.t: Hypoglycemia — administer oral glucose and transport
options.f: Incorrect. Blood glucose is 480 (very high, not low). The fruity breath and deep breathing indicate DKA, not hypoglycemia.

options.t: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) — support airway, provide high-flow oxygen if hypoxic (currently SpO₂ is adequate), position of comfort, and transport rapidly for IV fluids and insulin therapy
options.f: Correct. DKA requires definitive hospital care. Support ABCs and transport. Do not give insulin in the prehospital setting (outside EMT scope).

critical.explanation: DKA from insulin non-compliance with vomiting/dehydration. Transport for IV fluids, electrolyte replacement, and insulin therapy. Do not attempt to treat with oral glucose (would worsen hyperglycemia).

critical.scenario: You respond to a 55-year-old female with Type 2 diabetes who is confused and lethargic. Her husband reports she has had a urinary tract infection for the past week and has been eating very little. She takes metformin and glyburide. On exam, her blood glucose is 65 mg/dL, skin is cool and clammy, and she is confused but able to follow simple commands. She is able to swallow safely.
critical.question: This patient has hypoglycemia likely triggered by poor oral intake during illness. What is the most appropriate treatment?

options.t: Administer oral glucose gel (15-20 g) into the buccal pouch, recheck blood glucose in 10-15 minutes, and transport
options.f: Correct. She is awake and can swallow safely. Oral glucose is appropriate for this hypoglycemic patient.

options.t: She has Type 2 diabetes so hypoglycemia is not possible — no treatment needed; transport for the infection instead
options.f: Incorrect. Type 2 diabetics on sulfonylureas (glyburide) can absolutely become hypoglycemic, especially when eating poorly.

critical.explanation: Hypoglycemia in a Type 2 diabetic, especially on sulfonylureas with poor oral intake, requires prompt treatment. Oral glucose is appropriate since she is awake and can swallow safely.
